summary: Ronacher recounts building a “sloppy” XML parser to evaluate LLM output quality without strict XML validity getting in the way. After failed attempts to coerce a model into fixing existing parsers, he had Claude generate a new library from scratch, then iterated with prompts and added tests, CI, packaging, and even branding. The result isn’t pristine and likely has bugs, but it was good enough to unblock his work with minimal manual effort. He stresses this is not an endorsement for serious open source use, but an experiment in what’s possible today. He closes by raising the unresolved question of licensing AI-generated code and its implications for open source.
key_points: Invalid XML from models makes judging content quality hard, so he needed a tolerant parser.
Claude produced most of the library, tests, CI, packaging, README, and logo through iterative prompting.
The output is messy but functional, and it unblocked his immediate task.
He frames it as an experiment rather than a recommendation for serious OSS.
AI-generated code raises unsettled licensing questions.
money_quotes: I asked Claude to `ultrathink` and write me a proper XML library from scratch.
It wrote ~1100 lines of code for the parser.
It configured the entire Python package, CI, PyPI publishing.
It however works well enough for me right now for what I'm doing and it definitely unblocked me.
This isn't an endorsement of using models for serious Open Source libraries.
Treat it as a curious side project which says more about what's possible today than what's necessarily advisable.
Is that even valid when there's barely a human in the loop?
key_insight: AI can rapidly bootstrap usable tooling and infrastructure with minimal human effort, but its outputs are best treated as experimental—and they surface unresolved legal and quality questions.
